perturbation.original_text: 2. Definition . "Confidential Information" means any information, technical data, or know- how, including, but not limited to, that which relate s to research, product plans, products, services, customers, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing or finances, which Confidential Information is designated in writing to be confidentia l or proprietary, or if given orally , is confirmed promptly in writing as having been disclosed as confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Definition . "Confidential Information" means any information disclosed by the Discloser to the Recipient during the term of this agreement. This includes, but is not limited to, that which relates to research, product plans, products, services, customers, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing or finances. For avoidance of doubt, unless designated by Discloser in writing as 'Confidential' within 30 days of disclosure, it will not be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Confidential Information" includes information 'designated in writing to be confidential or proprietary, or if given orally, is confirmed promptly in writing'. The modified definition adds a 30-day limit for written designation, creating an in-text contradiction. The new definition is more limiting than the original definition. This contradicts the section on mandatory disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Mandatory Disclosure. In the event that the Recipien t or its directors, officers, employees, consultants or agents are requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information, the Recipient shall give prompt notice so that the Discloser may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief. In the event that such protective order is not obtained, the Recipient shall disclose only that portion of the Confidential Information which its counsel advises that it is legally required to disclose.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information' by adding a 30-day limit for written designation. The original definition did not have this limitation. This contradicts section 4, 'Mandatory Disclosure', because the recipient is only obligated to protect information designated as confidential. The new 30-day limit means information disclosed outside this window is no longer considered confidential, potentially requiring disclosure of information that would have been protected under the original definition and thus creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Non-Disclosure of Co nfidential Information . The Recipient agr ees not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not disclose any Confidential Informati on to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient who are required to have the information in order to carry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sign a Non-Di sclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each such person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed. Recipient agrees that it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Non-Disclosure of Co nfidential Information . The Recipient agr ees to make a commercially reasonable effort not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not deliberately disclose any Confidential Informati on to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient who are required to have the information in order to carry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sign a Non-Di sclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each such person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed. Recipient agrees that it will take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any intentional misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract uses strong language ('agrees not to use', 'will not disclose'). The modification weakens this language to 'agrees to make a commercially reasonable effort not to use' and 'will not deliberately disclose'. This introduces an in-text contradiction with the remedies section, which relies on the original strong non-disclosure commitments to justify injunctive relief, creating uncertainty in enforcement of the section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Remedies. Recipient agrees that its obliga tions hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. A ccordingly, Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.justification: The original text used strong language ('agrees not to use', 'will not disclose') while the changed text weakens this to 'agrees to make a commercially reasonable effort not to use' and 'will not deliberately disclose'. This directly contradicts section 10 (Remedies) because that section explicitly states that 'monetary damages would be inadequate' and that Discloser is entitled to 'injunctive relief'. By changing the original text to use less certain language like 'commercially reasonable effort', it introduces ambiguity and makes obtaining injunctive relief less certain, which is a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Term. The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of three (3) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Term. The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of three (3) years following the date of this Agreement, except with respect to information disclosed during Penetration Testing, which shall be protected indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: The original term is clearly defined as three years. Adding an exception for Penetration Testing data protected 'indefinitely' creates an in-text contradiction. It creates a conflict with the definition of 'Confidential Information', particularly around how long that information is actually kept confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Definition . "Confidential Information" means any information disclosed by the Discloser to the Recipient during the term of this agreement. This includes, but is not limited to, that which relates to research, product plans, products, services, customers, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing or finances. For avoidance of doubt, unless designated by Discloser in writing as 'Confidential' within 30 days of disclosure, it will not be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception to the three-year term for Penetration Testing data, stating it shall be protected 'indefinitely.' This directly contradicts Section 2, which defines 'Confidential Information' as information disclosed 'during the term of this agreement'. The original text set a clear three-year limit, but the changed text introduces an indefinite period for specific data. Since Section 2 specifies that information is only confidential if disclosed *during* the agreement's term (which was originally a limited 3 year term), and now Penetration Testing data is protected indefinitely after the term, it creates a contradiction of how long data is considered confidential.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the intern al laws of the State of California, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide. The federal and state courts within the State of California shall have excl usive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the intern al laws of the State of California, except for disputes regarding intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of Delaware. The agreement shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide. The federal and state courts within the State of California shall have excl usive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The section originally states California law governs all disputes. Introducing an exception for intellectual property disputes to be governed by Delaware law creates a contradiction. The exclusive jurisdiction clause in California is now contradicted by the potential application of Delaware law to IP disputes, which could imply Delaware courts have jurisdiction over those specific claims. This introduces ambiguity regarding which court has jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the intern al laws of the State of California, except for disputes regarding intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of Delaware. The agreement shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide. The federal and state courts within the State of California shall have excl usive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that California law governs all disputes and that California courts have exclusive jurisdiction. The changed text introduces an exception, stating that Delaware law governs intellectual property disputes. This creates a contradiction because the exclusive jurisdiction clause now conflicts with the potential application of Delaware law, which could imply Delaware courts have jurisdiction over IP disputes. This fits the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type because it makes jurisdiction unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Remedies. Recipient agrees that its obliga tions hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. A ccordingly, Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Remedies. Recipient agrees that its obliga tions hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, but does not expressly agree that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation may cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser may be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, only after proving actual damages. The recipient can be subjected to monetary damage.
perturbation.explanation: The original remedies clause asserts that monetary damages are inadequate and injunctive relief is available without proving actual damages. The altered remedies clause states that the recipient can be subjected to monetary damages as well, and requires proving actual damages for injunctive relief, directly contradicting the original assertion and reducing Discloser's legal protection, creating an uncertainty if only the monetary damage is the only solution. This weakened commitment regarding remedies contradicts the strong language used in other sections regarding the importance of protecting Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Non-Disclosure of Co nfidential Information . The Recipient agr ees to make a commercially reasonable effort not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not deliberately disclose any Confidential Informati on to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient who are required to have the information in order to carry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sign a Non-Di sclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each such person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed. Recipient agrees that it will take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any intentional misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 10 weakens the remedies available to the Discloser. The original text asserts monetary damages are inadequate and injunctive relief is available without proving actual damages. The changed text states the recipient can be subjected to monetary damages and requires proving actual damages for injunctive relief. This directly contradicts Section 3, which emphasizes the recipient's obligation to protect confidential information with commercially reasonable efforts and take reasonable measures to prevent disclosure. The weakened remedies in the changed Section 10 contradict the strong protective obligations in Section 3, creating a clear inconsistency.
